name: Pulmonary embolism (PE)
body:
**Pulmonary embolism** is the obstruction of the pulmonary artery or its branches by a thrombus, which in most cases originates from the deep venous system. The classic triad (pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, dyspnea) has low sensitivity; it should be suspected in cases of **unexplained acute dyspnea**, tachycardia, syncope, hypotension, or signs of DVT in the lower extremities. Risk factors include immobilization, recent surgery, cancer, pregnancy, oral contraceptives, and thrombophilias.

Stratification using clinical prediction rules (**Wells**, **GENEVA**) and D-dimer testing (high negative predictive value in low-probability cases) guides the diagnostic approach. **CT pulmonary angiography** is the gold standard in many centers; echocardiography is used to assess for right ventricular strain. Treatment includes **immediate anticoagulation** (unfractionated heparin or enoxaparin) and evaluation for thrombolysis or embolectomy in high-risk PE (shock or hypotension).

**Nursing:** administer oxygen therapy, perform hemodynamic monitoring, check for signs of bleeding following anticoagulation, apply compression stockings if not contraindicated, and provide patient education regarding treatment duration and signs of recurrence.

clinicalBox.title: Wells score — probability of PE
clinicalBox.content:
Clinical scoring (simplified version):
Clinical signs and symptoms of **DVT** — 3 pts
**PE** as the most likely diagnosis — 3 pts
**HR** > 100/min — 1.5 pts
Immobilization or recent surgery — 1.5 pts
Previous **PE or DVT** — 1.5 pts
**Hemoptysis** — 1 pt
Active **cancer** — 1 pt

**≤ 4:** low probability (perform D-dimer if applicable) · **> 4:** high probability → urgent pulmonary imaging.

Ministerio de Salud de la Nación Argentina. Estrategias y guías de atención.
American College of Chest Physicians. Antithrombotic Therapy guidelines.
